hook: Oldest Muslim burial ground at the Cape, packed with the graves of slave imams
facts: Granted to the Muslim community around 1805 after the Batavia administration permitted Islamic worship
Holds the kramat of Tuan Guru, who wrote a Quran from memory while jailed on Robben Island
Closed by the colonial authorities in 1886, triggering a riotous funeral protest down the slopes
Sits at the top of Longmarket Street on Signal Hill
w: Up at the top of the Bo-Kaap lie the earliest Muslim dead at the Cape: slaves, exiles, artisans, and the imams who built Cape Islam underground. Tuan Guru rests here, who copied out a whole Quran from memory on Robben Island. When the city closed it in 1886, the funerals turned to riot. Holy ground that fought back.

hook: Granite headstone for the only dog ever formally enlisted in the Royal Navy
facts: Just Nuisance, a Great Dane, was enlisted as an Able Seaman at HMS Afrikander in 1939
His conduct sheet logged offences including riding trains without a pass and sleeping in officers' beds
Buried with full military honours, a Royal Marines firing party and a bugler, on 2 April 1944
Grave sits atop Red Hill near the old SA Navy Signal School, reached by a steep flight of steps
w: Climb the steep steps above Simon's Town to a granite slab honouring a Great Dane. Just Nuisance enlisted in the Royal Navy as an Able Seaman in 1939, the only dog ever formally on the books, charged with riding trains without a pass and sleeping in officers' beds. Buried in 1944 with a firing party and bugler. Full honours, four paws.

hook: Shelves of boxed slave bones dug up by a property developer in 2003
facts: Builders striking foundations in 2003 cut into a forgotten burial ground of the colonial underclass
Bones were dated to roughly 180 to 270 years old, many likely enslaved people and the Cape poor
Around 2,500 numbered boxes of remains now line the shelves of a purpose-built ossuary
Stands at the corner of Somerset and Buitengracht Streets
w: Digging foundations for an apartment block in 2003, builders cut into an unmarked graveyard of the Cape's forgotten underclass: slaves, sailors, the poor. Work stopped amid furious protest, and around 2,500 numbered boxes of bones now line a small ossuary, each a person the city paved over. Reburial behind glass, with the receipts.

hook: Cape Town's second-oldest mosque hidden inside a Long Street townhouse
facts: Built as a residence around 1788, converted to a mosque in 1807 by freed slaves
Named for the date palms once growing in the front garden, one survivor still standing
Oldest substantially unaltered building on Long Street
Founder Jan van Bougies reportedly lived to 112, dying in 1846
w: Walk past it on Long Street and you would never guess: a flat-fronted townhouse that has been the city's second-oldest mosque since 1807, when two freed slaves bought the place after a quarrel over who should lead prayers. A lone date palm out front gave it the name. Founder Jan van Bougies supposedly made it to 112.

hook: A warehouse secretly run as a church when only Calvinism was legal at the Cape
facts: Merchant Martin Melck built it as a 'warehouse' in the 1770s because Lutheran worship was banned
Used covertly for services from 1771 before religious tolerance allowed open conversion
Sculptor Anton Anreith carved the swan finial and much of the woodwork in the 1780s-90s
Among the oldest church buildings in South Africa
w: With only Calvinist worship permitted, Cape Lutherans dodged the ban by raising a 'warehouse' on Strand Street and quietly holding services inside from 1771. Once tolerance came, the disguise dropped and sculptor Anton Anreith dressed it up, crowning the gable with a swan. A church that began life pretending to be a granary.

hook: Two 1790s cannon still fired at noon daily, claimed as the oldest guns in regular use
facts: Time signal first fired from the Castle in 1806, moved to Lion Battery on Signal Hill in 1902
Two 18-pounder muzzleloaders cast in 1794 are still loaded and fired six days a week
Each gun is charged with about 1.5 kg of gunpowder and rattles windows across the bowl
Triggered electrically from an observatory master clock since the 1860s
w: Every day except Sunday, the city flinches at noon when a pair of 1794 cannon on Signal Hill go off with a flat boom you feel in your chest. They have kept the city's time since 1806, once linked by wire to an observatory clock. Reckoned the oldest guns anywhere still fired daily. Set your watch, then duck.

hook: A demolished neighbourhood remembered on a giant floor map stitched by its exiles
facts: Founded in 1994 in a former Methodist church on the edge of the bulldozed District Six
Around 60,000 residents were forcibly removed after the area was declared white in 1966
Centrepiece is a hand-painted floor map where former residents marked their lost homes
Original street name signs, salvaged before demolition, hang inside
w: Apartheid declared this mixed inner-city quarter white in 1966, bulldozed 60,000 people out to the Cape Flats, and left a scar of empty ground. The museum fills an old Methodist church with salvaged street signs and a vast floor map where the exiled return to ink in where their houses stood. A neighbourhood kept alive in chalk and thread.

hook: The actual theatres where the world's first heart transplant happened in 1967
facts: Christiaan Barnard transplanted a heart into Louis Washkansky at Groote Schuur on 3 December 1967
Donor Denise Darvall was killed by a car while crossing the road to buy a cake
Museum opened in 2007 in the original operating theatres, with silicone figures recreating the night
Washkansky survived 18 days before dying of pneumonia
w: In a Groote Schuur operating theatre on 3 December 1967, Christiaan Barnard lifted a young woman's heart into a dying grocer and made medical history. The donor had been killed crossing the road to buy a cake. The recreated theatres, down to silicone surgeons, sit preserved where it happened. The recipient lasted just eighteen days.

hook: A 1673 toll and signal post, one of the oldest buildings in the country
facts: Erected by the Dutch East India Company in 1673, a year before the Castle was occupied
Began as a signal station, then a tollhouse taxing farmers heading to ships in the bay
Postholder Sergeant Muys lent his name to Muizenberg
Later a police station, stables, brothel and hotel before 1980s restoration as a museum
w: Squat and thick-walled on the Muizenberg main road sits a building older than the Castle, thrown up by the Dutch in 1673 to signal ships and tax passing farmers. A sergeant called Muys minded it and gave the suburb its name. Down the centuries it served as police post, stables, brothel and hotel before anyone realised what it was.

hook: A VOC fiscal's lavish 1770s mansion, now a quiet gallery of colonial watercolours
facts: Built around 1777 for Willem Cornelis Boers, the Company's chief prosecutor at the Cape
Name means 'rest and joy'; the street Buitenkant marks the old edge of town
Houses the William Fehr Collection of early Cape prints, drawings and watercolours
Carved teak doorway often attributed to sculptor Anton Anreith
w: Most visitors miss it: a grand double-storey townhouse built around 1777 for the Company's chief prosecutor, its teak doorway dripping with carving. Inside hangs a hushed collection of early Cape watercolours, the colony as settlers drew it. The name means 'rest and joy', rich coming from the man who ran the prosecutions.

hook: Edwardian municipal pool with the only public Turkish baths in the country
facts: Indoor swimming pool opened in 1908 at the upper end of Long Street
A Turkish bath suite was added around 1927, still the only public one in South Africa
Building mixes Victorian, Edwardian and Art Nouveau detailing and is heritage-protected
Hot rooms, steam and a marble plunge survive in near-original condition
w: Behind a grey Edwardian facade at the top of Long Street, a 1908 municipal pool still does laps, and through a side door waits the only public Turkish bath in the country, added in the 1920s. Hot rooms, steam and a marble plunge survive nearly untouched, brass doorknobs and all. A century of sweat and chlorine, mercifully undisturbed.

hook: A 360-year-old pear, propped on crutches, planted with the first Dutch settlers
facts: Brought from Europe and planted in the 1650s by Jan van Riebeeck's gardeners
Reckoned among the oldest cultivated trees in South Africa, over 360 years old
Named because its leaves once dyed wool a saffron yellow
A protected Champion Tree, held up by braces and still fruiting each autumn
w: In the Company's Garden, propped up on braces and poles like an ancient pensioner, stands a pear tree planted by Van Riebeeck's gardeners in the 1650s, around the founding of the colony itself. Over 360 years old and still dropping fruit each autumn, named for leaves once used to dye wool yellow. Older than nearly everything around it.
